metadata.title: UPSC Mains GS Paper 1 — Post-Independence India Comprehensive Syllabus
metadata.purpose: Standalone Post-Independence India file for MentorsDaily UPSC portal — AI module generation
metadata.note: Covers 1947 onwards — distinct from Modern History (colonial period). NCERT Class 12 Politics in India Since Independence is the backbone.

book_1.title: India Since Independence
book_1.author: Bipan Chandra
book_1.usage: Comprehensive post-independence political and economic history

book_2.title: Politics in India Since Independence
book_2.usage: Primary textbook — Nehru era, Emergency, coalition politics, regional parties

book_3.title: India After Gandhi
book_3.author: Ramachandra Guha
book_3.usage: Narrative history — partition, integration, democracy building

book_4.title: India: Social Change and Development
book_4.usage: Social transformation, caste, land reforms, urbanisation post-independence

modules.module_name: Partition, Independence and Integration of Princely States (1947-1950)

topics.topic_name: Partition of India — Causes, Violence and Refugee Crisis
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 1-2; Ramachandra Guha Part 1; NCERT Class 12 Ch 1
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India Since Independence — Ch 1: Challenges of Nation Building
key_concepts: Two-Nation Theory — Jinnah's demand for separate Muslim homeland
Cabinet Mission Plan 1946 — grouping scheme rejected by Congress
Mountbatten Plan (June 3 Plan) 1947 — partition and transfer of power framework
Radcliffe Line — Sir Cyril Radcliffe drew boundaries in 5 weeks
Punjab partition — worst violence — 2 million dead, 14 million displaced
Bengal partition — Calcutta riots, Noakhali violence, Gandhi's fast
Refugee crisis — largest voluntary migration in human history
Rehabilitation Ministry — Nehru government managing 8 million refugees
Communal violence patterns — urban vs rural, pre-partition vs post
Secular India's founding — Nehru's rejection of Hindu Rashtra option
Nehru-Liaquat Pact 1950 — minority protection in both countries
Economic division — canal headworks, railways, military assets split
Pakistan gets West Punjab, Sindh, Baluchistan, NWFP (Pakistan) + East Bengal
India gets rest — undivided majority of territory and population
Long-term legacy — Kashmir dispute, water sharing, trade barriers
key_personalities: Mountbatten — last Viceroy, partition architect
Cyril Radcliffe — boundary demarcation
Jinnah — Pakistan founder, two-nation theory
Nehru — first PM, secular India vision
Gandhi — inter-community peace, opposed partition emotionally

topics.topic_name: Integration of Princely States — Sardar Patel's Achievement
topics.primary_source: Ramachandra Guha Ch 2-4; V.P. Menon — Integration of the Indian States
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics Ch 1 — Challenges of Nation Building
key_concepts: 562 princely states — varying in size from tiny to Hyderabad (Nizam's dominion)
Doctrine of Lapse — historical precedent, but not used in 1947
Instrument of Accession — legal mechanism for states to join India
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel — Deputy PM, Home Minister — Iron Man of India
V.P. Menon — key bureaucrat who structured the integration process
Stand-still agreement — trade and communications during transition
Junagadh — Muslim ruler, Hindu majority — Pakistani accession reversed by plebiscite
Hyderabad — Operation Polo September 1948 — police action — Nizam surrendered
Kashmir — Maharaja Hari Singh's accession after Pakistani tribal invasion
Instrument of Accession signed October 26, 1947 — Indian troops airlifted
Travancore, Bhopal, Jodhpur — resistance to accession overcome by negotiation
Privy Purses — constitutional guarantee to rulers in exchange for accession
Privy Purses abolished 1971 — 26th Constitutional Amendment
Linguistic reorganisation of states — 1956 — Fazal Ali Commission
Success of integration — India remained unified unlike fears of balkanisation
key_personalities: Sardar Patel — master strategist of integration, threat and negotiation mix
V.P. Menon — draftsman of Instruments of Accession
Nizam of Hyderabad — last holdout, Operation Polo forced accession
Hari Singh — Kashmir's Maharaja whose delay caused the conflict
Sheikh Abdullah — Kashmir PM, aligned with India

sample_questions: Examine the role of Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel in the integration of princely states. (10 marks)
How was the Kashmir problem created and what has been its long-term impact on India-Pakistan relations? (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: Constitution Making and the Indian Republic (1946-1952)

topics.topic_name: Constituent Assembly — Debates and Constitution Adoption
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 3; Granville Austin — The Indian Constitution; NCERT Cl 12 Politics Ch 1
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Political Science — Indian Constitution at Work
key_concepts: Constituent Assembly — elected 1946, 299 members, chaired by Dr Rajendra Prasad
Objectives Resolution 1946 — moved by Nehru — framing vision for Constitution
Drafting Committee — chaired by B.R. Ambedkar — 7 members
2 years, 11 months, 18 days — time taken to draft Constitution
Debates on UCC (Uniform Civil Code) — not made mandatory, put in DPSP
Reservation debate — Ambedkar's insistence, Nehru's support for limited period
Language controversy — Hindi vs. regional languages — Article 343-351
President vs parliamentary system — why India chose Westminster model
Fundamental Rights modelled on US Bill of Rights
DPSP inspired by Irish Constitution
Federal with unitary bias — strong Centre design
Constitution adopted November 26, 1949 — came into effect January 26, 1950
Jan 26, 1950 significance — INC 1930 Purna Swaraj resolution
Dr Rajendra Prasad — first President of India
Granville Austin's assessment — Constitution: The Cornerstone of a Nation
key_personalities: B.R. Ambedkar — Chairman, Drafting Committee — 'Father of the Constitution'
Jawaharlal Nehru — Objectives Resolution, parliamentary system advocate
Sardar Patel — Fundamental Rights sub-committee
K.M. Munshi — key contributor to fundamental rights drafting
Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar — constitutional legal expertise

sample_questions: Discuss the major debates in the Constituent Assembly. How did they shape the Indian Constitution? (12.5 marks)
Why did India adopt a parliamentary system of government? Critically evaluate its functioning. (10 marks)

topics.topic_name: Nehruvian Economic Model — Planning, PSUs, Mixed Economy
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 5-7; NCERT Class 12 Ch 3 — Politics of Planned Development
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India — Ch 3: Politics of Planned Development
key_concepts: Nehruvian socialism — state-led development, not Marxist but inspired by Fabian socialism
Mixed economy — public sector for commanding heights, private sector for rest
Planning Commission established 1950 — Nehru as de facto chairman
Five Year Plans — First (1951-56) agriculture focus; Second (1956-61) heavy industry
Mahalanobis model — P.C. Mahalanobis — Second FYP — steel, machinery priority
Critique of Mahalanobis — agriculture neglect, capital goods imbalance
PSU creation — Steel Authority, BHEL, ONGC, HAL — commanding heights strategy
Industrial Policy Resolution 1956 — Schedule A (state monopoly), B (mixed), C (private)
Licence Raj — industrial licensing system — Entrepreneurs needed permits
Land reforms — abolition of zamindari — varied success across states
Community Development Programme 1952 — rural development through extension
Failure of CPD — elite capture, poor implementation — led to Panchayati Raj
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee 1957 — recommended Panchayati Raj institutions
Green Revolution seeds (Borlaug varieties) — introduced late 1960s after Nehru
Nehru's legacy — industrial base, IITs, IIMs, AIIMS, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre
key_personalities: P.C. Mahalanobis — Planning Commission architect, Mahalanobis model
V.K.R.V. Rao — national income data, planning economics
Nehru — vision of scientific socialism
B.R. Shenoy — lone critic of planning model, predicted inflation

topics.topic_name: States Reorganisation, Language Policy and National Integration
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 8-9; NCERT Class 12 Ch 7 — Regional Aspirations
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 7: Regional Aspirations and Ch 8: Recent Developments
key_concepts: Linguistic states demand — Potti Sriramulu's fast unto death 1952 for Telugu state
Andhra Pradesh created 1953 — first linguistic state
States Reorganisation Commission 1953 — Fazal Ali, Hridaynath Kunzru, K.M. Panikkar
SRC Report 1955 — linguistic principle accepted, 14 states and 6 UTs
States Reorganisation Act 1956 — major redrawing of state boundaries
Bombay controversy — Maharashtra vs Gujarat vs Bombay city — solved 1960
Punjabi Suba demand — Akali Dal — Punjab divided 1966 — Punjab, Haryana, HP
Official Language controversy — Hindi as sole official language — South India protests
Official Languages Act 1963 — English continued indefinitely for official use
Northeast reorganisation — small states carved out from Assam 1970s
Mizoram and Arunachal Pradesh — full statehood from UTs 1987
Jharkhand, Uttarakhand, Chhattisgarh — new states 2000
Telangana — 29th state 2014 — bifurcation of Andhra Pradesh
Demands for statehood — Vidharbha, Gorkhaland, Coorg, Bodoland (ongoing)
Constitutional provisions — Article 3 — Parliament can create/change/merge states
key_personalities: Potti Sriramulu — martyr for Telugu linguistic state
Fazal Ali — SRC chairman
Nehru — initially opposed linguistic states, later pragmatically accepted
Gobind Ballabh Pant — Home Minister, managed states reorganisation

sample_questions: Evaluate the Nehruvian model of economic development. Was it the right choice for India? (12.5 marks)
Examine the basis of states reorganisation in India. Should it be done on linguistic or administrative grounds? (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Nehru Era — Foreign Policy and Early Conflicts (1947-1964)

topics.topic_name: Non-Alignment Movement and Nehru's Foreign Policy Vision
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 10-11; NCERT Class 12 Ch 4 — India's External Relations
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 4: India's External Relations
key_concepts: Non-Alignment — not neutrality — independent foreign policy rejecting Cold War blocs
Panchsheel — Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence — India-China 1954
NAM founding — Nehru, Nasser, Tito — Belgrade Summit 1961
Bandung Conference 1955 — Afro-Asian solidarity, India's central role
Hindi-Chini Bhai Bhai — India-China friendship slogan — illusion shattered 1962
Sino-Indian War October-November 1962 — China's sudden attack
McMahon Line — 1914 — China never accepted; NEFA (now Arunachal) territory
India's military unpreparedness — civilian interference in military strategy
Nehru's health declined after 1962 — died May 27, 1964
Indo-Pakistan War 1947-48 — Kashmir — ceasefire January 1, 1949 — UN mediation
Nehru referred Kashmir to UN — later regretted — bilateral preferred
Goa liberation 1961 — Operation Vijay — Portuguese expelled by military
Nuclear policy — Nehru opposed nuclear weapons, promoted peaceful use
India-US relations — tensions over NAM, Pakistan alignment of US
Nehru's legacy in foreign policy — autonomy doctrine, strong influence in developing world
key_personalities: Nehru — architect of NAM and Panchsheel
K.S. Bajpai — first Secretary-General, Ministry of External Affairs
Krishna Menon — Defence Minister during 1962 defeat — resigned
Marshal Ayub Khan — Pakistan President, proxy war on Kashmir

sample_questions: Explain the policy of Non-Alignment. Is it still relevant in today's world? (12.5 marks)
Analyse the causes and consequences of the Sino-Indian War of 1962. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Political Transition and Indira Gandhi Era (1964-1977)

topics.topic_name: Lal Bahadur Shastri, Green Revolution and 1965 War
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 12; NCERT Class 12 Ch 2 — Era of One-Party Dominance
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 5: Challenges to and Restoration of Congress System
key_concepts: Lal Bahadur Shastri — PM June 1964 to January 1966
Shastri's simplicity — austerity, personal integrity as leadership style
Jai Jawan Jai Kisan — slogan combining military and agricultural priority
Indo-Pakistan War 1965 — triggered by Pakistan's Operation Gibraltar
Battle of Asal Uttar — India's tank victory — turning point
Tashkent Agreement January 1966 — USSR mediation — Shastri died that night
Green Revolution — Norman Borlaug wheat varieties — Punjab, Haryana, UP
IARI (Indian Agricultural Research Institute) — M.S. Swaminathan — HYV seeds
Food security achieved — India moved from ship-to-mouth to buffer stocks
Social consequences — Green Revolution benefited large farmers, bypassed poor
Indira Gandhi — PM 1966 — Congress old guard vs her 'syndicate' conflict
1967 elections — Congress weakened in states — coalition era beginning
Bank nationalisation 1969 — 14 major banks — populist move
Abolition of Privy Purses 1971 — 26th Amendment
Garibi Hatao slogan — 1971 elections — Congress landslide
key_personalities: Lal Bahadur Shastri — moral leadership, 1965 war
M.S. Swaminathan — Green Revolution architect in India
Indira Gandhi — Congress president, PM from 1966
Morarji Desai — Deputy PM, Congress old guard leader

topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 13-14; Ramachandra Guha Ch 24-26; NCERT Class 12 Ch 6
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 6: The Crisis of Democratic Order
key_concepts: Operation Searchlight March 1971 — Pakistan army brutality in East Pakistan
10 million refugees into India — massive humanitarian and security burden
Indira-Brezhnev Treaty August 1971 — India-Soviet Friendship Treaty
India-Pakistan War December 1971 — 13-day war
General Sam Manekshaw — COAS — India's decisive military victory
Surrender of 93,000 Pakistani soldiers — December 16, 1971 — Vijay Diwas
Bangladesh created — Sheikh Mujibur Rahman — Mujib's release from prison
Simla Agreement 1972 — bilateral resolution, PoW return, Shimla Accord
JP Movement 1974 — Jayaprakash Narayan — Bihar students, Total Revolution
Allahabad HC 1975 — found Indira guilty of electoral malpractice
Emergency June 25, 1975 — Article 352 internal Emergency declared
MISA — Maintenance of Internal Security Act — mass arrests
Press censorship — darkest chapter of Indian democracy
Forced sterilisation programme — Sanjay Gandhi — compulsory family planning
End of Emergency March 1977 — elections called — Congress lost — Janata victory
key_personalities: Indira Gandhi — Emergency declaration, authoritarian phase
Jayaprakash Narayan — Total Revolution, Janata coalition architect
Sam Manekshaw — military genius of 1971 war
Sanjay Gandhi — Emergency inner circle, forced sterilisation
Justice J.C. Shah — Shah Commission Report on Emergency excesses

sample_questions: Examine the causes and consequences of the Emergency (1975-77). What lessons does it hold for Indian democracy? (12.5 marks)
Assess India's role in the liberation of Bangladesh in 1971. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: Janata Era, Indira's Return and Rajiv Gandhi (1977-1991)

topics.topic_name: Janata Government, Indira's Return and Punjab Crisis
topics.primary_source: Ramachandra Guha Ch 27-30; NCERT Class 12 Ch 6-7
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 5, 7: Recent Developments in Indian Politics
key_concepts: Janata Party victory 1977 — first non-Congress government — Morarji Desai PM
44th Constitutional Amendment 1978 — restoring fundamental rights, limiting Emergency powers
Shah Commission 1977 — investigated Emergency excesses
Janata collapse 1979 — internal contradictions, Charan Singh brief PM
Indira Gandhi returned 1980 — Congress(I) landslide — Indira the comeback
Anandpur Sahib Resolution 1973 — Sikh autonomy demands — Akali Dal
Bhindranwale — Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale — Sikh militant, Punjab crisis
Operation Blue Star June 1984 — army entered Golden Temple to flush out militants
Deaths including Bhindranwale — Akal Takht damaged — Sikh outrage
Indira Gandhi assassination October 31, 1984 — by Sikh bodyguards
Anti-Sikh riots November 1984 — 3,000 killed in Delhi — Congress leaders implicated
Rajiv Gandhi PM — sympathy wave — 1984 elections — Congress 404 seats
Bofors scandal — kickbacks in howitzer deal — tarnished Rajiv
IPKF — Indian Peacekeeping Force — Sri Lanka — Tamil Eelam conflict 1987-90
Rajiv Gandhi assassination May 1991 — LTTE suicide bomb — election campaign
key_personalities: Morarji Desai — PM 1977-79, principled conservative
Bhindranwale — Punjab militant symbol
Indira Gandhi — assassination October 31, 1984
Rajiv Gandhi — computer revolution, IPKF, Bofors
Sam Pitroda — technology advisor to Rajiv, telecom revolution

sample_questions: Examine the Punjab crisis and Operation Blue Star. What were its long-term implications for national integrity? (12.5 marks)
How did Rajiv Gandhi's tenure both modernise and damage India's democratic fabric? (10 marks)

topics.topic_name: 1991 Economic Reforms — LPG and Narasimha Rao Government
topics.primary_source: Bipan Chandra Ch 20-21; NCERT Class 12 Ch 9 — Recent Developments
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Indian Economics — Ch 2: Indian Economy 1950-1990
key_concepts: 1991 Balance of Payments crisis — gold pledged to Bank of England
IMF conditionality — structural adjustment — forced liberalisation
P.V. Narasimha Rao — PM — Congress minority government, bold reform
Manmohan Singh — Finance Minister — architect of 1991 reforms
LPG — Liberalisation, Privatisation, Globalisation — reform package
Industrial delicensing — most industries freed from Licence Raj
Trade liberalisation — import quotas removed, tariffs reduced
FDI liberalisation — automatic route for most sectors
Disinvestment — partial privatisation of PSUs
SEBI — Securities and Exchange Board of India — capital markets reform
Exchange rate — rupee made convertible on current account
Mandal Commission implementation 1990 — V.P. Singh — OBC reservation 27%
Babri Masjid demolition December 6, 1992 — communal polarisation
Kargil War 1999 — under PM Atal Bihari Vajpayee — infiltration from Pakistan
Pokhran II (Operation Shakti) May 1998 — India declared nuclear state — sanctions
key_personalities: P.V. Narasimha Rao — architect of 1991 reforms from political side
Manmohan Singh — technocratic reform execution
Montek Singh Ahluwalia — Planning Commission — reform design
Atal Bihari Vajpayee — coalition PM, Pokhran II, Kargil, Vajpayee Doctrine

sample_questions: Critically evaluate the 1991 economic reforms. Were they a necessity or a choice? (12.5 marks)
Analyse the Kargil War 1999. What were its diplomatic and military lessons? (10 marks)

topics.topic_name: UPA Government — Rights Revolution and Economic Growth
topics.primary_source: NCERT Class 12 Ch 9; Economic Survey 2004-14 editions; India After Gandhi Part 3
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Politics — Ch 9: Recent Developments in Indian Politics
key_concepts: UPA-1 (2004-2009) — Congress + Left Support — Common Minimum Programme
NAC — National Advisory Council — Sonia Gandhi — rights-based legislation
MGNREGA 2005 — 100-day employment guarantee — largest social protection
RTI Act 2005 — transparency revolution — thousands of information requests
Forest Rights Act 2006 — tribal land rights
NFSA 2013 — food as legal right — 5 kg grain at subsidised price
India-US Civil Nuclear Deal 2008 — 123 Agreement — Left withdrew support
Manmohan Singh saved deal — trust vote won with alleged horse-trading
8-9% GDP growth 2004-08 — India's golden decade
Global Financial Crisis 2008 — India's stimulus, V-shaped recovery
UPA-2 (2009-2014) — scam era — 2G spectrum, CWG, Coalgate
Anna Hazare movement 2011 — Lokpal demand — massive public support
Lokpal Act 2013 — finally passed after anti-corruption movement
AAP formation 2012 — Arvind Kejriwal — new political entrant
UPA-2 lost popular mandate — 2014 elections — Congress historic defeat
key_personalities: Manmohan Singh — PM, liberaliser turned welfare-state architect
Sonia Gandhi — NAC chair, rights-based approach advocate
Aruna Roy — MKSS — RTI and MGNREGA civil society push
Anna Hazare — anti-corruption movement leader
A. Raja — 2G spectrum scam
Arvind Kejriwal — emerged from Anna movement, founded AAP

sample_questions: Examine the rights-based approach of the UPA government. Was it a paradigm shift in governance? (12.5 marks)
What led to the anti-corruption movement of 2011? Assess its impact on Indian democracy. (10 marks)

modules.module_name: NDA Era — Modi Government and Transformations (2014-2024)

topics.topic_name: Modi Government — Policy Initiatives and Controversies
topics.primary_source: Economic Survey 2014-24; MoSPI data; PIB releases
topics.ncert_reference: Current Affairs — Economic Survey, Budget documents, Ministry Annual Reports
key_concepts: 2014 elections — BJP 282 seats — first single-party majority in 30 years
Jan Dhan Yojana 2014 — financial inclusion — 51 crore accounts
Swachh Bharat Mission 2014 — ODF India declared 2019 — 11 crore toilets
Make in India 2014 — manufacturing sector push
Demonetisation November 8, 2016 — Rs 500/1000 notes banned — disruption vs benefit debate
GST July 1, 2017 — One Nation One Tax — unified indirect tax
RERA 2016 — Real Estate Regulatory Authority — housing sector reform
Ayushman Bharat 2018 — largest health insurance scheme
PM-KISAN 2019 — Rs 6000/year income support to farmers
Article 370 abrogation August 5, 2019 — J&K divided into two UTs
CAA 2019 — Citizenship Amendment Act — Muslim exclusion controversy
COVID-19 response 2020 — national lockdown, Vaccine Maitri
Atmanirbhar Bharat — self-reliance economic vision post-COVID
2024 election — NDA returned but BJP below majority — coalition dependent
Interim Budget 2024 — fiscal consolidation, infrastructure push
key_personalities: Narendra Modi — PM from 2014, BJP leader
Amit Shah — Home Minister — J&K, CAA
Nirmala Sitharaman — Finance Minister from 2019
Raghuram Rajan — RBI Governor 2013-16 — monetary policy reform

sample_questions: Evaluate the impact of demonetisation on the Indian economy. (10 marks)
Examine the key features of GST. Has it fulfilled its objectives? (12.5 marks)

topics.topic_name: Social Movements, Caste Transformation and Urbanisation
topics.primary_source: NCERT Class 12 Sociology — Social Change and Development; Bipan Chandra Ch 22
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Sociology — Ch 4: Change and Development in Rural Society; Ch 5: Change and Development in Industrial Society
key_concepts: Dalit movement post-Ambedkar — Republican Party, BSP, rise of Dalit politics
OBC politics — Mandal mobilisation — backward class assertion 1990s onwards
Women's movement — anti-dowry 1980s, anti-rape 1980s-2013, #MeToo 2018
Environmental movements — Chipko (1973), Narmada Bachao (1985), Silent Valley
Tribal rights movements — Jharkhand movement, FRA campaign
Green Revolution social impact — class differentiation in rural areas
Land reforms — zamindari abolition success, tenancy reform partial, ceiling laws weak
Urbanisation — 27% (2001) to 34% (2011) to 36% (2021) — slow but accelerating
Migration — rural-urban, inter-state — economic and social consequences
Middle class expansion — post-liberalisation — consumption economy driver
Right to Information Act 2005 — democratisation of information
Hindu nationalism — RSS, BJP — Ayodhya movement — transformation of political Hinduism
Minority politics — Muslim Personal Law Board, Shah Bano controversy 1986
LGBTQ+ rights — Section 377 struck down 2018 — social transformation marker
Social media and politics — 2014 onwards — new political communication
key_personalities: Kanshi Ram — BSP founder, Dalit political mobilisation
Medha Patkar — Narmada Bachao Andolan
Baba Amte — social service, leprosy work
Ela Bhatt — SEWA — women's informal worker organisation

sample_questions: Examine the impact of the Mandal Commission implementation on Indian society and politics. (12.5 marks)
Discuss the major social movements in post-independence India and their contribution to social transformation. (10 marks)

daily_routine.reading: 1.5 hours — Bipan Chandra + NCERT Politics Class 12
daily_routine.current_affairs_integration: 30 minutes — link historical events to contemporary issues

input_params.era_focus: nehru | indira | coalition | liberalisation | upa | nda | social

output_format.daily_target: topic_id + key dates and personalities flashcard
output_format.timeline_card: key events in chronological order for the era
